text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'll discuss how to fine-tune a large language model to use tools. I'll start with a high-level overview of what we're trying to do here and then walk through a concrete example with Python code. So, just to level set here, let's answer the question, what is fine-tuning? And all this is is adapting a model to a specific use case through additional training. The analogy I like to use here is to think of the base model as this raw stone that we've just extracted from the earth. And while it has a lot of potential, it in of itself isn't super useful. But what we can do with the base model is chisel and refine it into something that's a bit more aligned with our use case. So this is exactly what happens when the various AI labs train base models such as GPT3 or Llama 3.2 to base and then refine these base models to follow instructions in a process called instruction tuning. But we can go one step further. We can take these instruction tuned models which are already more helpful than the base model and we can make them even more helpful by fine-tuning them to do yet another behavior. Instead of just following instructions, we'll also fine-tune a model to use tools. So that's exactly what I'm going to walk through in this video. But how do we do this? The way we fine-tune models is the same way we train models. We give them examples to learn from. And so these examples are called our training data. In other words, these are illustrative examples of the behavior we want the model to learn. For example, if we have an instruction tuned model and we ask it something like what's 1,00 * 923.01 / 3 to the power of 4. Modern instruction tune models, they'll probably do something like this where they'll break down the calculation into steps. So they'll first do 3 to the^ four, then do the multiplication, and then divide everything. And while this will give better results than if the model just tries to answer this math equation in one shot, this is still not the right answer. This is one of the fundamental limitations of large language models. They're not really good at doing math or very precise calculations or tasks. And so this is not what we want our large language model to do. Instead, when it gets a question like this where we know it's not one of its strengths, we can have it call a tool. What this means is instead of having the model actually respond to this query, we're going to have it respond with a output with a special structure and then we can parse this structure and actually call this tool. In this case, we see the large language model is calling a calculator tool and it's passing in a query to this calculator tool, which is exactly what the user typed in. What we can do with this from a practical standpoint is anytime we see the large language model generate a structured output like this, we can parse it, see what tool it wants to call, and then we can write some Python code to execute that tool, generate an output, and then we can feed that output back to the language model so it can generate a helpful and accurate response to the user. And so this is the behavior that we want the model to do. So to pull this off, we're going to need to construct a bunch of examples of conversations like this so the model can learn this behavior. We'll need at least hundreds of examples, but more examples given that they're high quality is going to be better. Here's just an example of what this might look like. A trace is simply a full conversation between a user and assistant and might include other types of roles. So, here's an example of the examples that we're trying to generate in a llama style chat template. So, I know there's a lot going on here, but I'm going to break it down step by step. First thing is that we have these special tokens, which I've colored in green, and then we have various roles for each of these messages. And then we have the actual messages in white. We see llama has these special tokens of begin text, start header ID, end header ID. These are just identifying who is talking. So this is a system message which is you are a helpful assistant. And then there's a end token. Then another start header. Now the user is talking. And we have that same question. What's 1,00 * 923.1 / 3 ^ 4? Then we have another end token. And now it's the assistant's turn to talk. But instead of responding, the assistant is generating a function call. And so in the case of llama, this is what function calls look like. It'll start with a Python tag and then function calls will kind of look like Python code. So be like a module dot whatever method you're trying to call. So let's say we have a wolf from alpha tool available. We can do wolf from alpha call and then we can pass in our query. We'll have an end token. And now we have a special role called ipython which is where we can put the results of this function call. So we can have the function call be in this very structured JSON format. We'll have another end header. And now the assistant has this full context when it starts to generate an answer. And then it can come up with the right answer cuz it actually used a calculator to do this. And an important note here is that this initial assistant message and this IP Python message is not something we need to show to the user from the user's perspective. They just asked this question and then they got this response. These are the kinds of traces that we want to generate in order to fine-tune a model. So, we want hundreds or even thousands of these traces. To make this all more concrete, let's walk through a specific example here. I'm going to fine-tune Gemma 31B to use tools. Gemma 3 is a openweight model from Google. The version I'm using here has 1 billion parameters, which means it can run pretty easily on my MacBook. For all the training and everything, I'm going to be using the hugging face ecosystem. And all of this is going to be coded in Python. And so here's a quick overview of the key steps. The first thing we want to do is define the tools that we're going to use in our training data. And then from there, we can generate queries that require calling these tools that we've defined. And now that we have queries, we can generate these full traces. So including things like system messages, user queries, assistant responses, assistant function calls, the results of these function calls, and we'll have like different varieties of these conversations. And then once we have that, we can fine-tune our model on these traces. And then finally, we'll evaluate the model to see if it's actually improving on this ability to call tools. And I'll just call out that all the code for this project is freely available at this GitHub repo, which I've linked in the description. Also, the data set that I used and the fine-tuned model are freely available on the hugging face hub, also linked in the description. The first thing we want to do is define tools. So my thinking here is I wanted to define as many tools as possible because I didn't want to just define like one or two tools and risk the model only learns how to use those one or two tools and not generalizing to other types of tools. The way I approached this is I first brainstormed with Chad GBT and Claude separately and I even went kind of back and forth. So I'd get a list from ChatGBT and then I would pass that list to Claude and then I would pass it back to ChatGBT. Eventually, I came up with 40 different tool ideas. And these are things like a calculator, quering a SQL database, creating a calendar event, sending a Slack message, searching the web, doing datetime calculations, doing currency conversions. So, a lot of things that large language models may struggle with. Once I had these tool ideas, I had Claude via cursor generate me this tools.yaml YAML file where each tool had this structured metadata where we had its name, a description of it and then all its input parameters. So we had these parameters, these properties and then this tool the calculator tool took in a expression input which was a string and we had a description for each of the input arguments. So we have this tool metadata and then from here I just had Claude take the next step and implement all these tools in Python. And so the important thing here is that a lot of these tools will like require you know quering a SQL database or searching the web or getting the weather in a city. So in order to make these queries verifiable and easy to run, I created a bunch of synthetic data to actually make these function calls. So basically here I'm just like simulating the function call. I'm not actually grabbing the weather in San Francisco or something like that. I just have these things like pre-baked into this tools.python file. And if you want to check out the tools.yammo file or the tools.py file, they're both freely available at the GitHub repo. So you can read through all the function definitions. Now that we have the tools, we can generate synthetic queries for them. For all the queries, I used GPT4.1. And I actually created three different types of queries. I created queries that didn't require tool calls. And so this is really important because if I only fine-tune the model on examples that require tool calls, there's going to be a risk that the model will just call tools for everything and we'll just forget how to answer questions and just have regular conversations. So I generated synthetic no tool examples. I generated synthetic easy tool examples. So basically this is where a tool call is needed but it should be something pretty straightforward. So the model can directly from the user's query generate a tool call. It doesn't need to do any kind of reasoning before that. And then I had hard tool calls which also required tools but might require some thinking before being able to know which tool to select. So at the end of this process I had 600 total queries where I had 200 of each of these subtypes. And then if you're curious about the code I used to generate it. If you go to the GitHub repo, it's this file 1- generies.ip notebook. And so there you'll see the prompts and the code I used to get GPT4.1 to make all these queries for me. Now that we have the queries, we can actually generate these traces for them. And so since we had three different types of queries, the traces for these three types look slightly different. So for the node tool queries, the traces looked something like this. I had an initial user message which consisted of the highle system instructions like a system message and the tool metadata. The reason I didn't use a system role here is because Gemma only was trained on two roles. There's a user role and a model role. So unlike Llama which had those four different roles of system, user assistant and IPython, Gemma only has two. So I had to work within those constraints to make these traces. After these highle instructions, I inserted a query from this no tool query list, one of the 200 different no tool queries I have. And then I just generated a response because no tool call is required. I just had Gemma 3N E4B instruction tuned generate a response. I did this using together.ai. And the reason I didn't use Gemma 31B is because I couldn't find that hosted anywhere and I wanted to generate these pretty quickly and running inference of Gemma 31B on my laptop would be a lot slower. Then for the easy queries, the initial messages were exactly the same. So I had these instructions and tool metadata. Then I inserted the easy query, but instead of generating a response, I had GPT4.1 generate a tool call. So this was in a structured JSON format which we'll see on the next slide. And then I actually called the tool using the implementations that we talked about on the previous slide. Then I extracted the tool result and passed that as a user message. So the tool result was inserted into the context. And then finally I had Gemma 3N E4B it generate a response just like before. And then for the hard queries, the process was very similar, but instead of just having GPT 4.1 do a tool call, I highly encouraged it to write out its thought process first and then generate a tool call. I did this because I wanted to teach the model this behavior of not just doing tool calls directly from particular queries, but learning how to reserve some space before the tool calls to kind of think through the problem of understanding the user's intent of the tools provided in the tool metadata cuz this won't include just one tool. It might include one to 40 different tools. Selecting the appropriate tools and then figuring out how to use that tool. though reserving some tokens for the model to reason about that. But this didn't really work as expected. I actually had to drop these hard examples from the training data. And I did that because it just wasn't working with the hard examples. Like the model wasn't improving. And I think it's for a couple of potential reasons. One, the thinking tokens were generated by GPT 4.1. So maybe the way Gemma thinks and the way GPT thinks is fundamentally different. So maybe that was throwing it off. And then the second reason might be because the hard queries weren't actually that hard. There may have been some cases where the hard query was really an easy query that the model didn't need to generate any thinking tokens before doing a tool call. And then of course all this code is in the GitHub and this is the file name to check out. Let's look at a specific chat template. And so the special tokens for Gemma are pretty similar to llama. So there's a begin of text token. There's start header ID. So the first message is the user message. But then within the first user message, I added some structure. So I added these instruction tags and tools tags. I didn't define these as special tokens. These are just going to be strings to the model. But it does add some structure to the initial message. Put the system instructions here within the instructions tags. And then I put in a list of the tools with metadata within these tools tag. And so I actually varied this. So some traces had just one tool in them and maybe that was the correct tool to use. Other traces had all 40 tools and everything in between. The reason this is important is because in practical situations you might have dozens of tools that the model can choose from and you may not know what tool is going to be needed by the user at the outset. So the model needs to decide that at inference time. And then we have another user message. So I inserted the query here and then this actually continues. Then was the assistant message. If no tool call was required, the assistant would just respond. But if a tool call was required, I added these special tool call XML tags. And so the tool call was actually some JSON text that included the tool name and the input arguments. And so it's important that we have the tool calls within special tags because this is going to make it a lot easier to parse at inference time. So basically anytime we see this end tool call tag we know to stop inference execute the tool call get the result and then the result I would inject into this next user message and then this should be tool result not tool call. So I added these tool result tags in this next user message. And then finally the final assistant message has this full context of the tool call and the tool result. And then it can generate its final answer. And then I also encourage the model to put its final answer in these final answer tags because maybe it's generating a lot of tokens to reason about these things and we only want to display the final answer to the user. One note here is that hugging face has this apply chat template function which will automatically add the special tokens and put the chat in the proper format because the training data set I have has the traces as a list of dictionaries where each message is a dictionary with a role and the content and then messages are grouped together in a list. Laura allows you to fine-tune models by only training a relatively small number of parameters. Here are the hyperparameters I used here for LORA.
